findings.claim: In enable_allreduce_rms_fusion(), the parent gate `has_device_capability(90)` (>=90, i.e. any Hopper-or-newer arch including SM100/SM120) is replaced by `is_device_capability(100) or is_device_capability(90)` (exactly 90 OR exactly 100). This narrows the AR-RMS fusion enablement so that GPUs with capability >90 but !=100 (e.g. SM120 Blackwell RTX / future archs) no longer engage the fused all-reduce+RMS-norm path under default -O2/-O3, silently reverting them to unfused per-layer allreduce+RMSNorm on the per-decode hot path. This is a config/startup-time evaluation (called once during VllmConfig setup, not per-step), and appears to be a deliberate correctness scoping (fusion validated only on SM90/SM100), so it is a suggestion rather than a genuine unintended regression.
findings.suggested_benchmark: gpt-perf — config-gated regressions; run once with the feature flag at its OLD default and once at its NEW default (and once explicitly unset) — compare throughput and peak memory to detect silent path changes for jobs that never set the flag.

findings.claim: enable_allreduce_rms_fusion() now additionally requires `parallel_config.data_parallel_size == 1`, disabling the fused all-reduce+RMSNorm pass for any TP>1 + DP>1 deployment under default -O2/-O3. This forgoes the fusion (the fused path targets the per-layer TP allreduce+RMSNorm hot path) for TP+DP jobs. However it is an explicit correctness workaround for a broken TP-DP combination (issue #34458) and is evaluated once at config time, so it is a justified guard, not an unintended perf regression.

In `create_forward_context` (vllm/forward_context.py), the commit adds an unconditional per-forward-pass loop over `vllm_config.compilation_config.static_forward_context` that builds a filtered `remaining_moe_layers` list (list comprehension over ALL registered static layers — attention + MoE + mamba, which can be hundreds of entries for large models) plus an in-place `.reverse()`, and imports `FusedMoE` inside the function. This runs on every `set_forward_context` call, i.e. once per model forward / execute_model step (gpu_model_runner.py:3287), so the O(num_layers) Python filtering cost is paid on the per-step hot path even when torch.compile is disabled (eager). It is default-enabled and unconditional. Cost is small (pure-Python list build over layer count, no device sync), so this is a minor CPU-side overhead, not a device-level regression; but it is genuinely on the steady-state decode path. The function-local `from vllm.model_executor.layers.fused_moe.layer import FusedMoE` import is cheap after first load (module cache) but still adds per-call import-machinery lookup.

BatchDescriptor was changed from a NamedTuple to a @dataclass(frozen=True). It is used as dict keys in CudagraphDispatcher.cudagraph_keys and hashed/compared inside dispatch(), which runs per model step (gpu_model_runner.execute_model, eagle spec-decode). A frozen dataclass's auto-generated __hash__/__eq__ are pure-Python and slower than a NamedTuple's C-level tuple hash/eq, and the new code also calls dataclasses.replace() (which builds a fresh instance via reflection) 1-2x per dispatch instead of the lightweight relax_for_mixed_batch_cudagraphs() method. The per-step overhead is tiny (a few small dict lookups over a handful of int/bool fields) but is default-enabled whenever cudagraphs are on.
